summary: The optimal concentration of carbon in carbon steel hand instruments falls between 0.6 to 0.8%, ensuring effective sterilization through standard procedures such as steam autoclaving while maintaining material durability necessary for surgical tools. This range aligns well with established practices in biomedicine where precise control over metal composition enhances both performance and safety during use. To ensure these critical properties are met without compromising sterility or longevity, a narrow band around 0.7% has been widely adopted across various healthcare settings globally. Given no contradictory data was found among reviewed sources supporting other options more definitively than current consensus indicates otherwise, retaining selection 'C' stands justified based upon
